revision.text:
[[en:Fuyo Jingū]][[ja:扶余神宮]][[ko:부여신궁]][[simple:Fuyo Jingū]] {{Short description|Unfinished Shintō shrine in Korea}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | map_type = South Korea | coordinates = {{coord|36|17|13.29|N|126|54|56.77|E|display=title, inline|format=dms}} | deity = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ([[Hachiman]]),
{{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}},
{{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}},
{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} }} '''Fuyo Jingū''' ({{Nihongo2|扶余神宮}}) was a planned {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shintō shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Buyeo County|en|Buyeo County|qid=Q50247|lt=Buyeo|1=Buyeo County}}. It was intended to help with the [[:en:Japanese occupation of Korea|Japanese occupation of Korea]] and to be Ranked as a ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''. Construction started in 1939 but was never completed.{{Cite journal |first1=Michio |last1=Nakajima |date=2010 |title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/27822898 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=21–46 |jstor=27822898 |issn=0304-1042}}{{Cite book |last1=Shimizu |first1=Karli |title=Overseas Shinto Shrines: Religion, Secularity and the Japanese Empire |last2=Rambelli |first2=Fabio |date=2022-10-06 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-23498-7 |location=London |pages=145–152 |language=English}} It was highly significant in propaganda with many describing it as the second {{Nihongo|Shintō capital|神都|Shinto{{efn|Note the distinction between this, spoken with two short syllables, and {{nihongo|Shintō|神道}} (the religion), spoken with a long second syllable and written with different ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}''.}}}} after [[Ise Grand Shrine]].{{rp|151}} {{ill|Buyeo County|en|Buyeo County|qid=Q50247|lt=Buyeo|1=Buyeo County}} was the capital of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], which had a good historical relationship with Japan. The shrine was intended to mark the permanent incorporation of Korea into Japan{{Cite web |title=POINT 정책정보포털 |url=https://policy.nl.go.kr/search/searchEDSRetrieve.do?dbId=edskci&an=edskci.ARTI.9648140 |access-date=2023-04-04 |website=policy.nl.go.kr |language=ko}} under the principle of ''Naisen ittai'' (One body of Japan and Korea). It was built with an intention of being parallel to [[Kashihara Shrine]] with materials from all across the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}.{{rp|150}} Four ''kami'' were enshrined there: {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} ([[Hachiman]]), {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Kōgyoku|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}, and {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}. All were associated with the conquest of Korea. According to legend Empress Jingū delayed her birth to attempt to conquer Korea.https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/151473165.pdf The design was based on [[Kashihara Shrine|Kashihara Jingū]], aiming to be a key Japanese site. Debates arose on which deities to include. Resources from the empire were used in its construction. However, the shrine wasn't completed due to war disruptions. Samchungsa shrine, built in its place in 1957, was dedicated to people who resisted the Japanese conquest.{{rp|224}} == History == In 1938, [[:en:Zenranan-dō|Zenra-nan]] set up a shrine in each village, the only prefecture to do so. Most shrines worshipped Amaterasu Ōmikami, some with Emperor Meiji. In Zenra-nan, 175 shrines were for Amaterasu and 106 for both Amaterasu and Emperor Meiji. A few in Junten District honored Kunitama. At first, the {{ill|Governor-General of Chōsen|en|Governor-General of Chōsen|qid=Q483997|lt=Chōsen Governor-General|1=Governor-General of Chōsen}} didn't back the one-shrine-per-village idea. But by 1939, he did, leading to more shrines being built. Zenra-nan had the most with 247 shrines. He intended the shrine to rival the [[Kashihara Shrine]] 2600th anniversary renovation.{{rp|151}} The Chōsen Governor-General wanted to stress ''Naisen ittai'' (One body of Japan and Korea). The shrine was to combine Korean traditions with Shintō practices and underline Korea's role in Japanese history. The main deity was [[Amaterasu]], but Fuyo Jingū notably worshipped four ''kami'' – {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Saimei|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}, {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}, and {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} – significant to Korea. In 1945 construction ceased, and in 1957 {{illm|Samchungsa shrine|ko|부여 삼충사}} was built in its place, dedicated to three loyal retainers of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] who would have fought against {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}. It used the same materials Fuyo Jingū had been built with.{{rp|224}} Samchungsa shrine is within the {{ill|Baekje Historic Areas|en|Baekje Historic Areas|qid=Q20650483|lt=Baekje Historic Areas|1=Baekje Historic Areas}}, registered in 2015 as a UNESCO {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}.{{Cite web | title=Samchungsa Shrine |url=https://whc.unesco.org/en/documents/135770/ |access-date=2023-11-08 |website=UNESCO World Heritage Centre |language=en}}{{rp|224}} == Integration of Korean customs == In the 1930s, Korea started blending local customs with Shintō shrines, seen by some as an attempt at assimilation. But this was often hindered by the Chōsen Governor-General. Still, local officials in the 1930s tried connecting Korean communities to shrines, leading to a fusion of Korean and Shintō traditions. Initially, the Chōsen Governor-General suppressed many Korean customs. But, from 1933, with the Agricultural Village Promotion movement, there was a change. This aimed to transform Korean village shrine sites into Shintō-like sites. Many Korean prefectures began recognizing these sites, merging Korean and Japanese traditions. While Amaterasu was popular, Korean traditions played a role. Festivals included Korean practices like wrestling. Korean mayors, in traditional outfits, led ceremonies, showing a mix of Japanese and Korean customs. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Hachiman Faith}} [[simple:Fuyo Jingu]] [[en:Fuyo Jingū]] [[ja:扶余神宮]] [[ko:부여신궁]] {{wikidata link|Q11246131}} [[Category:1939 establishments in Korea]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in Korea]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Korea]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
